title: Immutability: The Rejection of Change
overview: In many modern paradigms, state is seen as the enemy. Immutability is the philosophy that once a piece of data is created, it cannot be changed. Instead of modifying an existing structure, we create a new one.
historical_context: Immutability has been a core tenant of functional programming since the Lambda Calculus, but it gained widespread adoption in mainstream systems as a reaction to the "Multicore Crisis" and the difficulty of managing shared mutable state.
mental_model: Moving from "how do I change this?" to "how do I transform this into that?"
key_innovations: Shared immutable state eliminates data races.
Persistent data structures for efficient updates (structure sharing).
tradeoffs: Increased memory usage (mitigated by structure sharing) and potential performance overhead compared to in-place mutation.
legacy: Influenced modern languages like Clojure, Rust, and even React state management patterns.
ai_assisted_discovery_missions: Explain the memory layout of persistent data structures (Hash Array Mapped Tries).
Compare the safety guarantees of Clojure vs. Rust in terms of immutability.
Trace the impact of immutability on modern concurrent database engines.
